content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of length NNN.

Find the **maximum length** of any subarray of AAA, such that, the bitwise OR of all the elements of the subarray is equal to the bitwise OR of the remaining elements of the array.

Report −1-1−1 if no such subarray exists.

Note:


A subarray is formed by deleting some (possibly zero) elements from the beginning of the array and some (possibly zero) elements from the end of the array.
The bitwise OR of an empty array is considered to be 000.

Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the length of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots,A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ representing the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the **length** of **longest** subarray such that the bitwise OR of all elements of the subarray is equal to the bitwise OR of the remaining elements of the array, or output −1-1−1 if no such subarray exists.
constraints: 1 <= N <= 5·10^5 (sum of N over all test cases ≤ 5·10^5)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible length of a subarray whose bitwise OR equals the bitwise OR of the remaining elements, or -1 if no such subarray exists.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: For no subarray of AAA, the bitwise OR of the elements of the subarray equals the bitwise OR of the remaining elements.
Test case 222: Consider the subarray [3][3][3] having length 111. The bitwise OR of the subarray is 333 and the bitwise OR of the remaining elements of the array is 1∣2=31 | 2 = 31∣2=3. Thus, the bitwise OR of all elements of the subarray is equal to the bitwise OR of the remaining elements of the array.
Test case 333: Consider the subarray [2,3,4][2, 3, 4][2,3,4] having length 333. The bitwise OR of the subarray is 2∣3∣4=72|3|4 = 72∣3∣4=7 and the bitwise OR of the remaining elements of the array is 777. Thus, the bitwise OR of all elements of the subarray is equal to the bitwise OR of the remaining elements of the array.

solution.approach: For each test case compute the total OR of the whole array. A subarray can be removed without changing this total OR iff every bit that is set in the total OR appears at least once outside the subarray. Therefore any position that is the *only* occurrence of some bit is "essential" – the subarray must not contain any essential position. The problem reduces to finding the longest contiguous segment that contains no essential indices. If there are no essential indices, any subarray of length N‑1 works (the remaining part must be non‑empty). If essential indices exist, the answer is the maximum distance between two consecutive essential positions minus one, also considering the prefix before the first essential and the suffix after the last essential. If the maximum length is zero, output -1.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N * B) per test case, where B = 30 (number of bits). Effectively O(N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the array and a boolean flag per position.
